question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to review the time limits for planning enforcement.
answer_text: The current time limits in relation to taking planning enforcement action are set out in section 132 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 and are standardised at 5 years across various breaches of planning control.  I have no plans currently to bring forward any review or amendment of the current time limits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the definition of major projects that her Department is applying when determining which infrastructure schemes are affected by the High Court judgment of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My Department does not apply a standalone or bespoke definition of “major projects” for the purposes of determining which schemes may be impacted by the recent High Court judgment relating to the A5 Western Transport Corridor.  I have asked officials to assess projects casebycase, where they have potential significant legal, financial and environmental risk while the appeal process continues.Taking decisions on other major projects at this stage could introduce uncertainty and increase the risk of similar legal challenges arising.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for pausing the Lagan Pedestrian and Cycle Bridge, Belfast.
answer_text: The issues being examined in the A5 case are relevant to approval of all of my departments major infrastructure projects and it is therefore considered appropriate not to make decisions on these projects until we have the clarity that final determination of these proceedings should provide. Taking decisions on other major projects at this stage could introduce uncertainty and increase the risk of similar legal challenges arising. This is not a position the Department has adopted lightly, and this will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will commission a review into the performance and culture of her Department.
To ask the Minister of Health how he will support research initiatives to encourage more people to work in the field of neurology and address current workforce shortages.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Review of Neurology report acknowledges the pressures within the current Neurology workforce. The need to grow and develop the workforce is identified as one of four key priorities in the report and has the largest number of recommendations. In order to support this growth, it is crucial to ensure that Neurology is regarded as an attractive speciality to work in. To that end, I recognise that research initiatives can positively contribute to interest in working in Neurology. A range of research initiatives are currently underway. These include an innovative clinical trial testing potential treatments for Motor Neurone Disease, active Neuromuscular research projects, a Headache trial and two Multiple Sclerosis research projects. Other measures identified in the report intended to increase interest in Neurology include increased exposure at early and middle grade training. It is intended that this will maximise recruitment to the Neurology Training Programme to support recruitment and retention within the medical workforce.In addition, a focus on multidisciplinary working, the development of career pathways and the establishment of Neurology Teams should also encourage more people to work in Neurology. It is important to recognise, however, that implementation of the Review’s recommendations would require over £65m in additional investment. Given that the draft multi-year budget proposed for my Department is far below what is needed, it is likely that difficult funding decisions will be required. The scale and pace of implementation will therefore have to be adjusted in line with funding.

answer_text: The Department for Finance (DoF) is responsible for recruitment across the NICS.  As such:My Department does not hold the information requested.  However, it is worth clarifying that it was 400 people brought into DfI, the majority of which were to fill existing vacancies during that period, not new roles; My officials are working with DoF colleagues to progress 41 recruitment competitions. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 20976/22-27, (i) for an update on the removal of the C1 licensing requirement for electric vans; (ii) to detail a timeline for the introduction of its removal; and (iii) whether its removal will be completed by summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he is working to address the threat of invasive species to biodiversity.
answer_text: Invasive species movements are a consequence of living in an increasingly interconnected world, and their management is a responsibility for all landowners and public authorities.  My Department’s primary focus is on increasing awareness to promote effective biosecurity and prevent the establishment of new invasive species.My Department takes forward regulatory action to comply with the EU regulation on invasive species. This includes work to reduce the distribution and numbers of Widely Spread Species, 12 named invasive species already well-established in Northern Ireland which impact on local biodiversity.Specific actions to address invasive species are detailed in the published Environmental Improvement Plan and proposed in the draft Nature Recovery Strategy, the latter including review of the Northern Ireland Invasive Species Strategy. DAERA also participates in awareness-raising activity such as the Invasive Species Northern Ireland website (https://invasivespeciesni.co.uk/), social media and the Check Clean Dry campaigns for reducing the spread of aquatic invasives, together with collaborative work across the British Irish Council administrations. An important delivery mechanism is the Shared Island Invasive Alien Species and Biosecurity initiative which sets out to deliver an enhanced programme of work around invasive alien species, working on a north-south basis. It is funded through the RoI Department of the Taoiseach Shared Island Initiative, with additional funding committed by the (RoI) National Parks and Wildlife Service and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency. Work commenced in 2024 and continues until 2029, aiming to promote the joined-up, strategic approach necessary to better address the threat presented by invasive species across the island of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the percentage of pothole repairs inspected; (ii) the percentage of repairs that were found to be inadequate; (iii) how long repairs were lasting on average, before needing to be repaired again, over the last three financial years; and (iv) whether there is a guarantee or warranty period, during which further repairs are required, without further charge.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.In addition to the contractual checks above, issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory. Temporary and long-term repairs, as distinct from structural resurfacing, are mainly delivered through Term Contracts for Small-Scale and Responsive Works. The quality and durability requirements for these repairs are set out in the Specification for Highway Works, alongside contract specific amendments.Durability is ensured through compliance with the specification, which requires the use of approved materials meeting relevant Standards, adherence to defined construction and workmanship requirements, and the application of inspection and testing regimes.Temporary repairs are intended to address immediate safety hazards until a permanent repair is completed. They are not designed to provide long-term durability, and the contract does not specify related time periods. Contractors must, however, ensure temporary repairs remain safe and effective for their short-term purpose.Permanent repairs are covered by a 52-week defect correction period, during which contractors must rectify any defects arising from their materials or workmanship.However, long and sustained periods of adverse weather, such as those experienced recently in the North, will have an adverse freeze thaw effect on the road network, impacting on the durability of both temporary and long term repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her engagement with the Northern Ireland Office on the impact of legacy costs on her Department’s budget.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee (TAC), established in August last year, has been leading and directing the reform of the curriculum.  Supported by 13 subject working groups, which are primarily made up of local teachers, academics and subject specialists, TAC’s role is to guide the development of a new, coherent, connected and knowledge rich curriculum for Northern Ireland.The TAC has met on 5 occasions and provides direct support and guidance to the working groups which were established in November.  Work is progressing as planned under the Committee’s strategic direction. My officials continue to work closely with the Committee to support this programme.I expect to receive the draft curriculum framework by the end of March 2026, and it is my ambition that phased implementation will begin in September 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will meet with the Minister of Health to discuss the findings of the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency report, Older car driver Killed or Seriously Injured (KSI) casualties in Northern Ireland, 2020-2024.
answer_text: I am aware of the report published on 13 November 2025, which uses PSNI road traffic collision data from 2020 to 2024 to review collisions involving older car drivers and identify any emerging issues in relation to the number of such drivers killed or seriously injured on roads here. My officials will require more time to fully consider the data presented. After this I can consider meeting with the Minister of Health.Road safety continues to be of paramount importance for my Department, and I am committed to working proactively, along with key partners in the Strategic Road Safety Forum to make our roads safer for everyone. As my resources are limited, I will continue to focus them on interventions where evidence shows they will have the greatest impact.

answer_text: I am aware of the report published on 13 November 2025, which uses PSNI road traffic collision data from 2020 to 2024 to review collisions involving older car drivers and identify any emerging issues in relation to the number of such drivers killed or seriously injured on roads here. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she will take, following the Older car driver Killed or Seriously Injured (KSI) casualties in Northern Ireland, 2020-2024, Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency's report, highlighting that 70 per cent of all KSI’s are occurring on roads with a 60mph speed limit.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. My Department is intending to launch a public consultation in the coming weeks on a Draft Speed Limit Review that will gather evidence from across these islands on a number of speed related matters, including KSIs relating to older car drivers.  My Department will consider the outcomes of this consultation that will inform a final Speed Limit Review Report which will be published in due course.   One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) the progress of subordinate legislation, following the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, to introduce the Graduated Driver Licensing; and (ii) when it is expected to be introduced.
answer_text: The statutory framework for the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) is set out in Part 3 of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and will require a suite of subordinate legislation to give if effect. Drafting of a commencement Order and three sets of Regulations, one of which will require Assembly debate, is advancing at pace. These Statutory Rules will provide for motorway driving for new and learner drivers, revised restrictions for newly qualified drivers, an approved programme of training and the production of a driver logbook. It will be important that the Order and the new subordinate legislation required come into force simultaneously.  Significant operational planning and development of the digital solution required to support delivery of the programme of training and logbook is also progressing well.  I expect to announce the anticipated date for the introduction of GDL shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Little Changes podcast is resourced using her active travel budget allocation.
answer_text: Audio production equipment was purchased in summer 2025 at a total one-off cost of 1,080.83 (excluding VAT). This is a minor expenditure in the context of overall spend on active travel. The ‘Littles Changes’ podcast does not incur any ongoing costs and is presented, recorded, produced and edited by one member of departmental staff in addition to their other duties and therefore at no additional cost to the department. Decarbonising transport and promoting cleaner and greener transport options are two of the department’s strategic foundations for a better future, and communication is a key part of that. Like many other organisations, the department is using the podcast format to bring the conversation to audiences in a different way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the local community responses to the proposed active travel routes in the Active Travel Delivery Plan consultation.
answer_text: I welcome the responses received to the public consultation on the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan.  A total of 346 responses were received, 294 from individuals and 53 from organisations which included representation from local interest groups.High level indications demonstrate that the majority of responses received endorsed the plan’s proposals to prioritise connections to local schools, town centres and public transport; and the high level design principles that are people centric, accessible to all, and prioritise safety. The detailed feedback received will be extremely valuable in informing proposals for the final plan.My officials are carefully reviewing the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan for my approval in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department determines the timing for pedestrians crossing at road crossings, over the timing for vehicles.
answer_text: The calculation of signal timings requires the consideration of a range of factors including:the number of various vehicular and pedestrian movements to be facilitated.the most efficient means of combining conflicting and non-conflicting movements into a sequence (known as a cycle).The allocated time within a cycle to the various movements so that the overall cycle time matches that of nearby junctions to facilitate coordination.Determining the length of periods between conflicting movements (known as intergreens) to ensure the safe passage of vehicular and pedestrian traffic.Other factors such as road geometry, traffic volumes, traffic speed. More detailed information is available in Chapter 6 of the Traffic Signs Manual.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the procurement process for the audio equipment used for the Little Changes podcast.
answer_text: The audio equipment was procured in accordance with public procurement policy for minor expenditure. A number of quotations were obtained, and the cheapest one was taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she is taking improve rural access to public transport, to reduce barriers to food access, as highlighted in the Rural Routes to Food Consumer Council for Northern Ireland November 2025 report.
answer_text: My Department’s draft Transport Strategy sets out a new vision and strategic priorities for our transport system through to 2035 aiming to support economic growth, regional balance, and improved connectivity across both urban and rural areas. The public consultation closed at the end of September 2025, and officials are now analysing responses to help shape a system that meets the needs of all communities.My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities. Both the Translink website and App can provide transport options from any location, address or stop, urban and rural, where transport links exist.  The website and app allow for address-to-address planning, park & ride planner and a bike it option.  I have allocated an additional 41% in 2025/26 to support the rural Dial-a-Lift transport service in all areas in the north which improves access to local goods and services, helping people travel independently and connected to their local communities.  The level of funding was commensurate with the level of funding requested by those providing this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is aware of any concerns raised, by those with disabilities, regarding access to Hill Street, Belfast, during the pedestrianisation experimental scheme.
answer_text: The Traffic Orders to be introduced as part of the pedestrianisation of Hill Street are in accordance with RSPPG E063 – Measures to Regulate Traffic. As part of the enactment of this policy, an overarching Section 75 screening form was completed.  I can confirm that my officials, as part of a consultation process, engaged with key business stakeholders, Cathedral Quarter Business Improvement District, IMTAC and the PSNI. In addition, a public consultation opened on 15 October 2025 and closed on 6 November 2025 which gave any interested party the opportunity to comment as part of the statutory procedure.During the development and consultation phases of the Hill Street Pedestrianisation scheme specific local issues relating to Section 75 groups were identified. Officials have considered and addressed the issues in advance of the introduction of the experimental scheme.As the pedestrianisation of Hill Street is being introduced as an experimental scheme, running for an initial 6 month period, any interested party has the opportunity to make further representations which will be fully considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to extend the Translink concession pass criteria, to include those who have not been refused a driving licence but are unable to drive consistently, due to dynamic disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the money collected from (i) parking fines; and (ii) speeding fines, are allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is measuring the impact of the Share the Road to Zero campaign.
answer_text: Share the Road to Zero is an initiative which aims to reduce road deaths by encouraging all of us to think about our shared responsibilities as road users, to be aware of our surroundings, and take more care when out and about. Once subscribers have signed up to Share the Road to Zero they receive weekly road safety advice and information from the Highway Code NI. Our key message is, as always, reduce your speed, wear a seatbelt, pay attention, ignore the phone and never ever drive having taken alcohol or drugs and drive.Share The Road to Zero currently reaches 33,367 newsletter subscribers. On social media, the campaign has built a strong presence with 36,000 followers on Facebook, 4,148 on X and is actively growing its audience on Instagram.In the last 30 days on Facebook, Share The Road to Zero has had a reach of 1.7 million. The two best performing posts were ‘Just Because’, which is about pedestrian safety and another on the use of brake lights for drivers. Within the past two years, sporting organisations and individuals, business and road safety organisations and local councils have lent their support to the initiative which has helped its messaging reach new audiences in different ways.A strategy has been devised to support the Share the Road to Zero campaign. It is embedded within our Road Safety Strategy to 2030.  The strategy sets a target of reducing road deaths and serious injuries by 50% by 2030, with a long-term goal of zero fatalities by 2050.  The action plans include performance indicators and progress tracking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has agreed proportions of road safety campaigns, targeting (i) drivers; (ii) pedestrians; and (iii) cyclists.
answer_text: Priorities are determined by evidence and statistics on the causations of KSIs (Killed or Seriously Injured) rather than setting fixed proportions of budget available to particular road user groups. The budget is allocated to areas which the evidence suggests will give us the greatest return, in terms of reducing the number of people killed or seriously injured on roads here. It is also allocated to protect those road users who are more vulnerable, such as pedestrians, cyclists, children and motorcyclists, and to promote lifesaving seatbelt wearing, if involved in a road traffic collision. Key causation factors reported by the PSNI for 2024/25 are Careless Driving, Drink/Drug Driving, and Speeding. Vulnerable road users, particularly pedestrians, children, older adults, and motorcyclists remain disproportionately affected. For example, over the five-year period 2020-2024, pedestrians alone accounted for 18% of KSI casualties.Based on this evidence, I initially prioritised the reinstatement of pedestrian and motorcyclist safety campaigns and have since launched the Anti-Drink Drive campaign. Evidence also demonstrates a growing problem involving those who take drugs and choose to drive. For that reason, I approved the commissioning of a new Anti-Drug Driving campaign, which aired last week for the first time.The evidence also shows that more than 9 out of 10 road traffic collisions are due to road user error, with drivers being responsible for the majority of crashes; speeding remains a major contribution to collisions. I have therefore approved two further new campaigns ‘Anti-Speeding’ and ‘Driver Behaviour’, these will air in early 2026. A new radio edit highlighting the dangers children face when getting on and off buses aired on 17 November across radio stations.Our media plan has not yet been fully finalised as the road safety problem continues to be monitored, but, in addition to those campaigns already mentioned we expect to address careless/inattentive driving, mobile phone use, seatbelt wearing and cyclist safety by the end of this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30928/22-27, (i) for an update on the report into the commissioning of ADHD services; and, (ii) to detail what date it will be published.
