question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions that have taken place with the UK Government regarding (i) funding for Casement Park; and (ii) capital support to other sports in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ANSWERI have had several discussions with both the NIO and Treasury in relation to funding for Casement Park. This includes meetings with the Secretary of State, Hilary Benn, as well as with the new Chief Secretary to the Treasury and his predecessor.  In these meetings I reiterated my desire to see the project move forward as quickly as possible and emphasised to the British Government the need for it to live up to its commitments in relation to the funding of Casement Park.As part of these meetings I also highlighted the Executive’s wider capital requirements and discussed potential costs relating to the 2035 FIFA Women’s World Cup.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the findings in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Active Travel in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail the actions she will take to ensure active travel spend is used wisely to deliver active travel projects.
answer_text: With respect to the Member’s first question, I welcome the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report into active travel in Northern Ireland. I can confirm that the report will be considered very carefully and a response to the recommendations will be provided in line with the established process. With respect to the second question, my department has taken a number of important steps in recent years to increase capacity to support the effective delivery of active travel projects.  This work has included the development of a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan, including proposed network plans for our major towns and cities. Officials are currently reviewing responses to a public consultation on that plan with a view to publishing the final plan later this year. When complete, the Active Travel Delivery Plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide a firm basis for the prioritisation and delivery of active travel infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond.Other steps that have been taken include filling key posts within the Active Travel team and increasing supply chain capacity to facilitate an increased future delivery pipeline.  My officials will continue to work diligently to ensure robust financial governance arrangements are in place for the delivery of active travel projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the work he is doing to improve welfare for companion animals.
answer_text: In May, I published my Animal Welfare Pathway, which is represents the most ambitious package of reforms for this area for over a decade. This includes several key initiatives which have the potential to transform companion animal welfare, including: a review of dog breeding and canine fertility services; regulation of the sale and supply of pups and kittens; and consideration of issues associated with a ban on aversive training devices. Over 480 responses were received to my consultation document containing proposals to regulate of the supply and sale of pups and kittens, which also outlined my aspiration to ban third party sellers.  My officials are continuing to analyse the submissions received and I hope to be able to publish a formal consultation response soon, which I will use to set out my final way forward. An expert advisory group has been established to review the operation of the current dog breeding regulations here and examine the provision of canine fertility services. Work is well underway, and I understand that the group has been gathering evidence and has met with a wide range of stakeholders. Finally, my officials have commenced initial policy development work on the remaining reforms in the pathway and have been examining the case for mandatory microchipping of cats, regulation of rescue and rehoming organisations and exploring potential changes to the operation of the current animal boarding regulations. While this work is in an early phase, I still hope to be in a position to initiate a public consultation on all these topics around the end of this year. These actions demonstrate my ongoing commitment to strengthening protections for companion animals across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the review of penalty fines for speeding will be complete.
answer_text: Officials are considering options for a review of penalty fines for speeding.  Once I have received briefing and advice from them I will consider the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the amount of a penalty fine for (i) on-street parking; and (ii) speeding, were decided.
answer_text: The current level of the fixed penalty for speeding was established in 2001, and while the specific rationale behind the original amount is not readily available, it reflects decisions made at the time by the relevant policy authorities.  I had recently instructed my officials to undertake a review of penalty fines for speeding, and  officials are currently considering the next steps and way forward.   The original cost of a Penalty Charge Notice (£60) was increased to £90 in 2012 through a decision taken by the Assembly. This change was introduced to act as a deterrent to those who do not adhere to parking restrictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why speeding penalty fines are not included in the Road Safety Strategy.
answer_text: The Road Safety Strategy to 2030 is an overarching document that identifies the Strategic Priority areas we – government, organisations and road users – must focus our efforts on in order to overcome challenges and deliver on the strategic outcomes connected with Safe People, Safe Roads and Safe Vehicles. The annual action plans that accompany the Strategy reflect the current and emerging trends in road safety. These actions are developed and lead by my and other departments and our road safety partner organisations in the emergency services. I have recently received briefing and advice from officials on a number of speeding related measures, including a review of penalty fines for speeding.  Officials are now considering the next steps and way forward. A new Action Plan is currently being developed and will reflect this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the report into the commissioning of ADHD services; and (ii) when it will be published.
answer_text: The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the provision of new laboratories for Forensic Science Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 7 - AQO 2362/22-27		  ANSWERThe project to develop a new forensic science laboratory and office accommodation on the Seapark site in Carrickfergus is progressing well.  The Full Business Case was approved in June 2025, and a Contractor was appointed in July 2025 to take forward the detailed design and construction phase.  Following an initial period of pre-construction design, construction is expected to commence in early 2026.  Forensic Science Northern Ireland is a vital component of the criminal justice system. It provides robust scientific services in support of justice for all.  With this investment, we are making sure it has suitable accommodation within which to continue to provide this crucial public service given the increasing sophistication and technological advancement of the techniques that the organisation deploys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, of the total budget set aside for construction of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme during the 2025-26; and 2026-27 financial years, to detail what capacity there is to allocate some of this budget over that two-year period to road safety messaging.
answer_text: The budget for the A5 scheme in the 2025-26 financial year is an Executive Flagship project, as such any need to surrender budget in 2025-26 will need to be made to the Executive for re-prioritisation.I am carefully considering the implications of the judgement in the A5 case and it is too early to identify the budgetary implications of the ruling. My priority is to find the right solution that sees the A5 built as quickly as I can so that no more lives are lost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, of the total budget, set aside for construction of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme during the 2025-26; and 2026-27, financial years, to detail the amount which may now be re-allocated to the construction of bypasses at an advanced state of planning, such as Cookstown, Enniskillen and Ballynahinch, in order to enhance air quality in those towns.
answer_text: I am carefully considering the implications of the judgement in the A5 case. The judgement is lengthy and complex and raises a number of issues that we need to deal with. At this stage, it is too early to identify the budgetary implications of the ruling but my priority is to find the right solution that sees the A5 built as quickly as I can so that no more lives are lost.Budgets have not yet been agreed for the 2026-27 financial year, however, this remains an Executive flagship project, and I am determined to deliver it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the share of the budget, set aside for construction of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme between Strabane and Ballygawley over the (i) 2025-26; and (ii) 2026-27 financial years, that will now be allocated towards safety improvements at known accident black spots on the existing A5.
answer_text: I am carefully considering the implications of the judgement in the A5 case. The judgement is lengthy and complex and raises a number of issues that we need to deal with. At this stage, it is too early to identify the budgetary implications of the ruling, but my priority is to find the right solution that sees the A5 built as quickly as I can so that no more lives are lost. Budgets have not yet been agreed for the 2026-27 financial year, however, this remains an Executive flagship project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget set aside for the construction of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme between Strabane and Ballygawley during the (i) 2025-26; and (ii) 2026-27, financial years.
answer_text: The DfI budget for the A5 scheme for the 2025-26 financial year is £165m which is now subject to review. Budgets have not yet been agreed for the 2026-27 financial year, however, this remains an Executive flagship project. Annual budget allocations to the scheme will be subject to Executive and Assembly approval as part of the statutory budget process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the reasons for not including condition-specific pathways as a year one priority in the Regional Review of Neurology Services.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on legislation relating to the Utility Regulator.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the creation of an active travel stakeholder group.
answer_text: I recognise the benefits for my department of positive constructive engagement with key users and stakeholders. As part of my commitment to this, an active travel stakeholder group will be established later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what statutory first aid training is required for new care home staff prior to commencing work.
answer_text: Under the Care Standards for Nursing Homes (2015) and Residential Care Homes Minimum Standards (2022), all new care home staff are required to undertake appropriate training as part of their induction process. While there is no single statutory course mandated prior to commencing work, care home providers must ensure that staff are trained to a level that enables them to respond effectively to the needs of their residents.The Care Standards do not explicitly state that First Aid training must be completed prior to commencing work. However, they do require that:An induction programme is in place for all staff, including bank and agency staff, and that this induction is specific to their role, The induction should ensure that staff are competent to carry out their duties, which includes responding to emergencies appropriately.The standards, issued by the Department of Health, stipulate that care home providers must ensure that at all times there are sufficient numbers of staff on duty who are trained in First Aid. This includes ensuring that new staff receive first aid training either before starting work or as part of their initial induction, depending on their role and responsibilities.In addition to training, the Care Home Minimum Standards also specify that all care homes in Northern Ireland must have appropriate First Aid policies and procedures in place and adhere to these at all times.The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) monitors compliance with these standards during inspections, and providers are expected to maintain up-to-date training records for all staff.The Department continues to work with stakeholders to ensure that training requirements reflect best practice and support the safety and wellbeing of residents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan 2025-2027 will help facilitate economic prosperity in the agri-food sector.
answer_text: I am passionate about the continued success of our Agri-Food Sector. It is our largest manufacturing sector, with a gross turnover of approximately 6 billion pounds and employs an estimated 80,000 employees across agriculture and food processing. It is particularly significant to our rural and coastal economies.  My Executive colleagues and I are committed through the Food Strategy Framework Action Plan to focus our collective efforts to maximise the opportunities our food system offers. Our focus in the next two years is to build collaboration and purposeful partnerships across departments and with key stakeholders to build on the good work already underway and to identify gaps and future opportunities. I recognise our economy needs thriving food and drinks processing, hospitality and tourism sectors. Improving productivity through high-value products and reducing inefficiencies, adopting new technology and investing more in innovation and in a highly skilled workforce will help drive competitiveness. Attracting and keeping talent will be critical, and we must work collectively with stakeholders to deliver and promote rewarding, secure and sustainable career opportunities.Although policy responsibility for many of these areas lies with the Department for the Economy, Mission 3, within the Action Plan sets out a number of actions we can take collectively to further develop economic prosperity. These include a review of knowledge and skills provision for the agri-food sector, effective signposting of the ranges of support services available to food and drinks processing businesses, optimising uptake of research and development opportunities, and export sales opportunities. I look forward to working collaboratively with my Ministerial colleagues to progress this exciting agenda.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when Translink will enable online applications and renewals for the Senior SmartPass.
answer_text: My Department is working alongside Translink to facilitate the move to online applications, which is due to be implemented before the end of 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Driver and Vehicle Agency will enable online applications for a full driving licence.
answer_text: DVA has introduced several online application services in recent years which include applications for a full driving licence. An online application process is also available to applicants applying for a first provisional driving licence; renewing an existing driving licence; replacing a driving licence; and notifying a change of address.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) how the appointment of board members to the Strategic Investment Board are determined; (ii) how transparency is ensured; and (iii) what oversight and accountability mechanisms are in place.
answer_text: The positions of Chair and Non-Executive Director of the Strategic Investment Board are public appointments regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments NI (CPANI).  New appointments are made following a publicly advertised open competition in line with the CPANI Code of Practice and are subject to oversight by CPANI.  The names of the successful candidates are published.Officials are currently preparing a recruitment competition for a Chair and Non-Executive Directors, which we expect to launch in the near future.The Chief Executive of the Strategic Investment Board may be appointed as an Executive Director on an ex officio basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the contribution of wastewater discharges in reducing water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support the Housing Executive provides to victims of domestic violence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s Domestic Abuse Action Plan seeks to ensure staff can provide support to those experiencing domestic abuse and to equip staff with the knowledge to signpost to appropriate services when required. The Action Plan acknowledges that domestic abuse can be a contributing factor in homelessness wherein the Housing Executive has a statutory duty to provide temporary accommodation to homeless applicants under the Housing (NI) Order 1988. This duty is met through a range of options for those experiencing domestic abuse, including Women’s Aid refuges, which provide temporary accommodation and support for women and children experiencing domestic and sexual abuse. Applicants’ support needs are considered when determining the suitability of accommodation and, in all cases involving domestic abuse, the safety of the Applicant is the primary consideration.  Additionally, the Supporting People Programme helps vulnerable people live independently in the community, including those affected by domestic abuse. This programme grant funds the delivery of short-term accommodation-based services, providing support for those in housing need and through short-term Floating Support services for those who remain at home or in a community setting. Individuals experiencing domestic abuse who are ineligible for assistance, or are not otherwise owed the full housing duty under the Housing (NI) Order 1988, can avail of advice and assistance from the Housing Executive, which can include information regarding self-referral hostels and referrals to floating support services as outlined above. The Housing Executive continues to be the main funder for such provision for those experiencing domestic abuse across Northern Ireland.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline when he will repeal Article 4(1)(c) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the interdepartmental working group will publish its updated paper on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of achieving the target of 80 per cent electricity consumption from renewable sources by 2030.
answer_text: Question 15 AQO 1824/22-27One of my four critical objectives for the Economy is to reduce carbon emissions; generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change.We must build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to facilitate delivery of the 80% by 2030 target. Whilst my Department has legislative responsibility for the target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any recourse patients have when a private medical practice closes prior to the competition of diagnosis, assessment or treatment.
answer_text: If a service user/patient is dissatisfied with the standard of care and treatment (or lack thereof) from a provider that has now ceased operating they may share their concerns with the relevant professional regulatory body (for example: GMC, GDC, NMC, NISCC, HCPC); the provider's medico-legal indemnifier; or commence legal action against the provider.If the service was registered with RQIA it would treat this as intelligence to determine if there was sufficient evidence of a breach of the relevant regulations or minimum standards, and consider any follow up action required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the safeguards for patients who purchase private (i) diagnosis; (ii) assessment; and (iii) treatment in the event of a private practice closing.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland provides publicly funded health and social care, including general medical services, primarily for the ordinarily resident population of Northern Ireland.  The Department does not provide private healthcare. Eligibility to access publicly funded healthcare in Northern Ireland is based on ordinary residence.  A person will be ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland when that residence is lawful, voluntary, and for a settled purpose as part of the regular order of their life for the time being, whether of short or long duration.  A person who is ordinarily resident is entitled to access publicly funded healthcare.Individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care system.In Northern Ireland, independent clinics and hospitals, which meet the criteria defined in the Independent Health Care Regulations 2005, are required to be registered with the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA), the body responsible for the regulation (registration, inspection and enforcement) of healthcare establishments and agencies in Northern Ireland.  Once a private practice closes, the RQIA ceases to have a role.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the provisions made to support the timely delivery of Departures from Standard, given the increased number of applications.
answer_text: It is worth clarifying that there has not been a significant increase in the number of applications for Departures from Standard (DfS), although the workload in this area does change depending on the nature and complexity of projects and the quality of submissions.To support the DfS process the Department has clear and structured application forms to ensure the provision of the information required. There is a specific branch identified as responsible for ultimate approval of DfS. We have moved to an electronical process to minimise the administrative burden of hard copy applications. In addition, when we are dealing with multiple applications from an Engineering Consultant or developer, where possible, my Department works with the applicant to establish scheme priority for processing DfSs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given her Department’s prioritisation of strategic road improvement schemes, to detail the impact this prioritisation has had on the regional balance of Departures from Standard applications and issuance of road licence agreements.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as Engineering Approvals and Road License Agreements. While I am committed to taking forward Strategic Road Improvements schemes as set out in our Emerging Transport Plans, and the City and Growth deals, these are not unduly prioritised and staff in my Department are working very hard to keep the many important services for which I have responsibility functioning for the benefits of all citizens. Departures from Standard and Road License Agreements are normally processed in chronological order, however there are occasions when the Department will prioritise an application if we are made aware that it is urgent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of members of staff in her Department that have been seconded to support the Departure from Standard process for strategic road improvement schemes.
answer_text: There are no seconded staff supporting the Departure from Standard process for strategic road improvement schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average processing time for the issuance of road licence agreements for each of the last seven years.
answer_text: My Department does not record the average processing time for Road Licence Agreements. Any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Divisions and this would produce data of limited accuracy given that some Road Licence Agreements can take a long time to agree due to factors outside the control of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average processing time for the (i) review; (ii) approval; (iii) and signing of a Departure from Standard within her Department for each of the last seven years.
answer_text: My Department does not record the average processing time for the review, approval and signing as each Departure from Standard (DfS) is unique a scheme and there are many issues that will impact on DfS processing times, including:Their submission as a package of departures, as these are returned only when all have been reviewed;The impact of legal, planning or financial implications for private developments may result in their application being placed on hold and not reviewed until all issues are resolved, which could be months or years;Poor quality of submissions, which often results in longer review times and the need for significant comments to be addressed and for applications to be amended and resubmitted; andDepartures from Standard are normally processed in chronological order, however there are occasions when the Department will prioritise an application if we are made aware that it is urgent. Staff availability and vacancies in the Divisions and Engineering Approvals team also impact on processing times, however, when possible, priority is being given to those applications that are highlighted as urgent by the applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when she plans to remove the written counterpart to the Northern Ireland driving licence; and (ii) when she will put in place any necessary consequential mechanisms to identify penalty points and other matters.
answer_text: The DVA has made significant enhancements to the Driver Licensing System since its introduction and is currently reviewing and prioritising further developments. An assessment of the work needed to support the abolition of the paper counterpart will form part of this review. The timescale for the delivery of these enhancements will be dependent on the availability of resource to take this forward, including the engagement with key stakeholders to ensure any consequential issues are addressed.The legislative framework to enable the replacement of the statutory function of the counterpart is in place, but the relevant law will require a Commencement Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to work by his Department to assess demand for a commissioned ADHD service, to detail (i) who was allocated to carry out this work; (ii) how they are carrying out this work; and (iii) the stakeholders they have engaged with to date.
answer_text: (i) The Department of Health has recently commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  A lead has been appointed through the HSC Leadership Centre, and the work commenced in early February, with an estimated completion date of end June 2025.  (ii) Work will be progressed in line with the agreed Terms of Reference for the assignment and will include a literature review and consideration of best practice in other jurisdictions; an assessment of existing ADHD service provision, including barriers and challenges; and an analysis of future demand to inform recommendations for a future commissioned service, including estimated costs, workforce requirements and supporting implementation plan.A list of stakeholders has been developed which includes representatives from the Department of Health, HSC Trusts, the Community and Voluntary sector and people with lived experience, amongst others.  Stakeholder engagement will be taken forward in the coming weeks to inform the findings of the assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the criteria that was applied to refuse funding to ADD-NI from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26; and (ii) the options open to ADD-NI to replace this funding.
answer_text: The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives, and included the following scoring criteria:evidence of how an organisation supports the department’s strategic objectives;evidence that an organisation is involved in carrying out relevant activities;evidence that an organisation will deliver clear outcomes, including how those will be measured and the targets that will be met;evidence that an organisation has delivered similar outcomes previously; evidence that an organisation has suitable monitoring and control systems in place, and suitably qualified and experienced staff; andevidence that an organisation will work in partnership across the statutory, community and voluntary sectors. These scoring criteria are listed and explained in the guidance for applicants, available on the departmental website.My officials will be in contact with all applicants to offer feedback, which I hope will be of assistance in applying for future funding rounds. Indeed, those who have missed out this time will have another opportunity later this year to bid for funding in 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the pressures facing the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what learning they have applied following the violent attacks and disorder during the summer of 2024.
answer_text: We have taken learning from other programmes which deal with societal issues, and believe a strong focus on a public health style approach is needed if we are to tackle racism and racial inequality. A public call for views was launched on 6th February to gain evidence for the new strategic approach to racial equality when the current strategy ends in December 2025.We want to hear from key stakeholders, the minority ethnic sector, and wider society about what they believe the root causes of racism and race hate are. We would encourage everyone to have their voices heard in this important consultation.Work is also ongoing to undertake a review to fulfil the commitment made by the Assembly after its urgent recall and statements of condemnation, following the racist attacks last year

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the remaining life of the existing rail stock; (ii) when new rail stock will be ordered; and (iii) when the new rail stock will be in operation.
answer_text: It is typical to replace rail stock when it is around 30 years old. The Enterprise fleet will be 30 years old in 2027; the Class 3000 fleet will be 30 years old in 2030-35, with the Class 4000 fleet reaching this age in 2040-41.  Translink and Irish Rail are currently procuring new Enterprise trains. It is anticipated that a contract will be awarded before the end of the year subject to Business Case and governance approvals being achieved.It is anticipated that the new Enterprise trains will come into service in 2029.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what work he is undertaking with the Minister of Health to reduce levels of ill-health related economic inactivity.
answer_text: Question 14 – AQO 1523/22-27Economic inactivity is an important policy area, led by the Minister for Communities.  I am committed to working with him and Executive colleagues, including the Minister for Health, to help address barriers to employment.  We are working closely with the Department for Communities on the Executive’s Disability and Work Strategy which has this issue at its core.  My Department plays a key role through provision of skills programmes which aim to make skills and education more accessible, including for those experiencing additional barriers such as disability or illness.  We also recently consulted on a Good Jobs Bill which aims to help those with ill health, and those caring for dependents suffering from ill health, to remain within or access the workforce.In addition, the social enterprise action plan’s focus on leadership support will increase the capacity of the sector as it continues to support those furthest from the labour market into meaningful work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how her Department is working to help people move away from group violence and gangs.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 12 - AQO 1477/22-27		ANSWERThe Department of Justice currently hosts the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC). This is a truly cross-sectoral initiative. The Programme works across Northern Ireland through statutory and community organisations to support people and communities that experience paramilitary harm. A key part of this is helping those who want to move away from involvement in paramilitary activity. Significant outcomes are being seen across the Programme. On top of major disruptions to criminal groups, we are seeing reductions in youth violence, and in paramilitary style assaults and public order offences. This Programme has also been at the forefront of work on trauma informed approaches and child criminal exploitation.In response to evidence gathered by EPPOC that showed the lack of specific initiatives to help people exit groups, the Programme has completed a feasibility study of what is called the Community Initiative to Reduce Violence (CIRV). This started in Boston, USA and has been used successfully in Scotland and England. It aims to identify and support those who genuinely want to move away from, or avoid joining, violent gangs. Following the completion of a feasibility study, a project team is working towards the implementation of a 12-month pilot phase in South and East Belfast, due to commence in April 2025. Through collaborative partnerships with community organisations and statutory partners, the initiative aims to create targeted support systems to help individuals desist from reoffending by helping them safely move away from gang involvement and group violence. CIRV is aimed at young people, aged 18-24, and will take a personalised problem-solving approach, encouraging participants to make positive life changes through the support of, for example, mentorship programmes, and education and employment opportunities. It will draw on already successful initiatives in EPPOC helping to join up existing supports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering the removal of the C1 licence requirement for electric vans, in order to bring Northern Ireland in line with Great Britain on the issue of licence derogation.
answer_text: The introduction of similar licencing changes here would be subject to the outcome of a public consultation process.  The Department plans to commence work on this as soon as resources allow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18800/22-27, when he will publish the analysis undertaken by policy areas in his Department which informed the review conducted in November 2023 into whether the Highway Code for Northern Ireland should be updated.
answer_text: An internal review of the Code was commissioned in November 2023 by all policy areas in the Department.  The Department does not plan to publish the analysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 11597/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will commission scalp reconstruction surgery.
answer_text: My Department commissions surgical care on a speciality rather than procedure basis.  Scalp reconstruction surgery can be a clinically required response to immediate or post trauma injury, cancer or congenital malformation. It may also range in complexity from wound management through to highly specialist reconstructive techniques requiring input from a number of specialities.  My Department is unaware of any specific issues in accessing clinical care across these services as outlined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink is exploring the introduction of batteries to retrofit existing trains on the network to reduce both carbon emissions and fuel costs.
answer_text: The current Translink railway fleet is not suitable to facilitate a battery retrofit to the vehicles. However, Translink have informed me that all new trains purchased in the coming years will encompass battery and/or electric propulsion technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Climate Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the active travel amendment in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, (i) whether his Department has defined what 10 per cent of the transport budget represents; (ii) how he will fund this figure; and (iii) when these figures and plans will be published.
answer_text: In defining the overall transport budgets, my department is working on the basis of including all the transport related functions of the department, including all expenditure in Transport and Roads Asset Management group as well as expenditure on public transport, which is predominantly Translink, and transport policy. In the current financial year, £20.7m has been directly allocated to Active Travel capital projects and resource budgets. In addition to this allocation, my department routinely undertakes many activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists that are not currently included within these figures. Officials are carrying out analysis of overall expenditure relevant to Section 22 of the Act for future reporting. My department is working on the basis of ramping up overall active travel investment to meet Section 22 of the Climate Change Act obligations by 2030. A number of important steps have been taken to increase capacity to support the delivery of active travel including filling key posts within the Active Travel team and the appointment of an Active Travel Support Office. My department has also developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan, which is currently open for public consultation. When complete, this plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (2022) and the Strategic Plan for Greenways (2016) to provide a firm basis for the future prioritisation of active travel infrastructure investment and delivery.  The delivery of the obligations will be subject to the availability of funding through the normal Executive budgetary processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18962/22-27, (i) for a breakdown of the £0.5 billion figure cited by (a) resource; (b) capital; and (c) earmarked projects, taking account of the alterations to the final opening budget allocations from the Equality Impact Assessment; (ii) to detail the percentage of the £0.5 billion allocated to improve wastewater treatment; (iii) to detail the wastewater treatment works that received this funding; and (iv) how he plans to fill the funding gap.
answer_text: (i) (a),(b) The table below sets out the breakdown of the £0.5 billion figure, including the additional £11.5m resource DEL and £19.5m capital DEL allocated following the October monitoring round:Capital 2024-25 £k EU Peace Plus (ring fenced) * 875 General allocation 343,167 Total 344,042 Resource 2024-25 £kTotal 149,230  *This is to cover the Department’s match-funding element of the EU Peaceplus grant for this scheme.(c)   The funding is earmarked for the delivery of water and wastewater base maintenance and capital enhancement as determined under the Price Control and subject to budget availability. The capital funding I have provided in addition to the final opening budget will be used to alleviate capacity constraints to enable housing connections. (ii) NI Water has advised that, of the £0.5 billion allocated, 33% overall has been allocated to improve wastewater infrastructure. (iii) NI Water has advised that they are investing public funding in the following Wastewater Treatment Works in 2024-25:Ballyronan WwTW Carrickfergus WwTW Lisnaskea WwTW - Cat 5 Killygonlan WwTW Loughries WwTW Clogher WwTW Bellaghy WwTW Kinnegar WwTW Lough Macrory WwTW Grange WwTW Ballygowan WwTW Garrison WwTWs Ballintoy WwTW Newcastle WwTW Tertiary Treatment Appraisal Belleek WwTW Ballyvoy WwTW Drumaness WwTW Monea WwTW Kilrea WwTW Downpatrick WwTW Tamlaght WwTW Markethill WWTW feasibility Dromore WwTW Ballygawley WwTW Robinsonstown WwTW Dromara WwTW LWWP - Belfast WwTW - Phase 1 Ancillary Work Moy WwTW Newry WwTW LWWP - Whitehouse WwTW Cabragh WwTW Meigh WwTW LWWP - Belfast WwTW Phase1 upgrade Dungannon WwTW Carrickfergus WwTW LWWP - Greenisland WwTW Waringstown WwTW Loughries WwTW Lisnaskea WwTW - Cat 5 Annsborough WwTW Kinnegar WwTW Clogher WwTW Dundrum WwTW Phase 2 Ballygowan WwTW Lough Macrory WwTW RWwIP PC21 Newcastle WwTW Tertiary Treatment Appraisal Garrison WwTWs Aghanloo WwTW Drumaness WwTW Belleek WwTW Culmore WwTW - Cat 5 Downpatrick WwTW Monea WwTW Mountfield WwTW Dromore WwTW Tamlaght WwTW Pomeroy WwTW Dromara WwTW  (iv)  I continue to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment, I am reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions and I have secured Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly. This legislation will provide powers to encourage increased use of sustainable drainage systems which use nature to separate and release storm water more slowly into the system so that it does not overrun wastewater treatment works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will prioritise delivery of road safety education in the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: There are already opportunities throughout the Northern Ireland curriculum for teachers to deliver road safety education.  At primary level, the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding Area of Learning requires teachers to enable pupils to develop knowledge, understanding and skills in keeping themselves healthy and safe.  At post-primary level, the Learning for Life and Work Area of Learning requires that pupils should have opportunities to “develop preventative strategies in relation to accidents in the home, school and on the road”.Additionally, The Department of Infrastructure provide educational road safety resources which primary and post primary schools can avail of.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when he first read the Story of Belfast Lough document; and (ii) for his assessment of it.
answer_text: The ‘Story of Belfast Lough’ document was brought to my attention by my officials following its publication on NI Water’s website. The significant water quality issues highlighted  have been known for some time and were identified in my Department’s Living With Water in Belfast Plan, which was published in November 2021 following Executive endorsement.  The document outlines some of the further work funded by my Department through this Plan since 2021, including integrated environmental modelling to inform the future design standards for the upgrades needed to the wastewater treatment works that discharge into Belfast Lough.It was always acknowledged that additional funding would be required to deliver all aspects of the Belfast Plan within the original 12-year timeframe.  In the New Decade New Approach agreement, published in January 2020, Living With Water was identified as a beneficiary of increased funding, however, this was not received. In 2023, NI Water also increased the cost estimate of its elements of the Belfast Plan by £700 million, pushing the overall costs from £1.4 to £2.1 billion.  An affordability review conducted by my Department in response to this increase has concluded that the need for the Belfast Plan continues to exist, however, without the necessary budget, its original timescale is no longer achievable. Delivery of the projects in the Belfast Plan will therefore be taken forward by my Department and NI Water as normal business at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets.  In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding, however, I will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 18339/22-27, for a breakdown of the cost implications for reinstating the Primary Modern Languages Programme.
answer_text: As per my response to AQW 18339/22-27, given the current financial pressures facing education at this time, I have no present plans to reinstate the Primary Modern Languages Programme.  As such, current cost implications have not been analysed.  The programme last ran in 2014/15 with funding of £900k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has undertaken an assessment of the risk of not updating the Highway Code for Northern Ireland to reflect recent changes in Great Britain.
answer_text: No single policy area of DfI has responsibility for the content of the Highway Code.  Rather, a review of the Code is commissioned periodically, and individual policy areas are asked to ensure that content relating to their area remains accurate. The Code is then updated as necessary, based on responses received.The most recent review was commissioned in November 2023, following the substantive update to the Highway Code in GB in 2022.  Policy areas were asked to consider the content of the Code and advise whether any amendment was required. The responses received resulted in no significant adjustments to the published version of our Highway Code.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether his Department will deliver the target of 80 per cent electricity consumption from renewable sources by 2030.
answer_text: Question 14 – AQO 1226/22-27One of my four critical objectives for the Economy is to reduce carbon emissions; generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change.We must build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to deliver the 80% by 2030 target. Whilst my Department has legislative responsibility for the target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to re-instate the Primary Modern Languages Programme.
answer_text: Given the current financial pressures facing education at this time, I have no present plans to reinstate the Primary Modern Languages Programme or another programme of this nature.  Modern languages have, however, been identified as a priority area of focus within the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  My officials continue to engage with colleagues in the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment to consider how best to support languages and encourage the development of language skills in young people.In addition, the Independent Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum that I have commissioned will make a series of policy recommendations regarding the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of modern languages teachers employed in the last five years, broken down by (i) full-time employment; (ii) part-time employment; and (iii) constituency.
answer_text: The Department of Education does not collect information on the subject taught by teachers and therefore is unable to provide the number of modern languages teachers employed in the last five years.The General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) is currently running its data collection which includes the main subject taught by teachers and they expect to have this information available by March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how the Department will deliver up to 1,400 new build social homes in the 2024-2025 financial year given constraints in the wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: Following confirmation of the extra budget for the Social Housing Development Programme in the October monitoring round, it will be for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, as the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, to assess how the additional budget may best be used to meet identified housing need before any final starts figure for 2024/25 can be confirmed or revised target set. I am advised that work on this is underway.Deliverability of any additional social home schemes in this financial year will be a key consideration for the Housing Executive in their deliberations regarding additional budget.In terms of social housing delivery, my Department has previously received confirmation from the Department for Infrastructure that when developments are already underway, with valid planning permission, and where sewerage/water planning conditions have been followed, NI Water is obliged to provide connections to the sewerage system and will endeavour to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to permit all taxis to use certain bus lanes in Belfast City Centre.
answer_text: The closure of Durham Street is necessary for the development of Belfast Grand Central Station and the associated public realm works.  My Department has been working closely with Translink to manage traffic flow during construction, and I am aware of the level of disruption  being experienced at certain times.To alleviate some of this disruption, and to support the taxi industry, I am bringing forward proposals which will permit Class A and Class C taxis to use certain bus lanes in the vicinity of the works.The taxi industry provides an important service within our community ensuring people, including the most vulnerable in society, have door to door access to work, education, socialising and medical appointments.  Tourists, particularly those using the new station, are also regular users of taxis.Work on the legislation is ongoing and I will announce further details on the commencement of the pilot in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he plans to develop and publish a workforce plan for GPs.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs.  I also recognise the importance of a sustainable workforce for general practice and the need to build and sustain the GP workforce.The Department of Health welcomed the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy which made a number of useful recommendations. My Department has taken forward a range of actions to address workforce and workload challenges, many of which are in line with recommendations set out in the RCGPNI Retention Strategy plan.  These include:  Taking speedy action to progress the 2023/24 Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Board recommendation for GPs and applying a 6.0% uplift for GP pay.  In addition, a 6% uplift for practice staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift for other expenses (3% recurrent) were also applied.     Agreeing the new 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract.  A key aim of these new contract arrangements is to provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices.  Focussing on building the GP workforce through funding a number of initiatives, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, GP Retainer Scheme, GP Mentor Scheme and GP Fellowship Scheme.In September this year, my Department commissioned a second Fellowship Scheme for GP Practices in the Southern Trust area designed to attract salaried GPs to work in Practices which they have taken over due to contract hand-backs.  The first round of recruitment has completed; with a second round now being worked through by the Federation.    Increasing the annual number of GP training places from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 (an increase of 86%).  Working with NIMDTA on innovations to the local GP training programme with the objective of enhancing the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.In relation to GP training numbers, the GP Training Group, which includes the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) and GP representatives including RCGPNI, will make recommendations on the number of GP training places required for the 25/26.  It will also progress work to project the number of GP training places required over the next 5 years.

answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will introduce a new Building Safety Act.
answer_text: My officials are currently working with major stakeholders to co-design a building safety policy for Northern Ireland.  I hope to bring this to the Executive in early 2027, with a view to introducing a Building Safety Bill at the start of the next Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the provisions that currently exist for the management of epilepsy in educational settings.
answer_text: Children and young people with medical needs, including epilepsy, should have a healthcare plan in place.  The responsibility for drawing up a healthcare plan lies with the relevant Allied Healthcare Professional (AHP) involved in the healthcare of the child or young person with epilepsy.  The AHP provides a detailed plan which is agreed with the parents and school and can be used as all or part of an individual’s Medication Plan.  Schools have responsibility to make reasonable adjustments to ensure that that the needs of pupils with medical needs, including epilepsy, are met.  The department provides the following guidance to support schools “Supporting Pupils with Medication Needs” at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/support-pupils-medication-needs. To ensure that school staff have a basic understanding, can recognise symptoms and seek appropriate support, awareness training should be completed.  This will help ensure that the needs of pupils can be met.  Epilepsy Action provides a free training module for schools.  The training required, however, for individual children will be noted in the medication plan.  The child and their family should be known to the Paediatric Epilepsy Nursing Specialists team who can provide specific support, if required. The Education Authority (EA) Special Educational Needs (SEN) Inclusion Service Medical Needs Service operates a central point of contact for schools to support the effective inclusion in schools of children and young people with medical needs, including epilepsy.  The service can be contacted at 028 7127 2340 or EAMedicalNeedsSupport@eani.org.uk.  Information and signposting to resources and training is also available on the EA’s website Support available to schools for Medical Needs | SEND Plan.

answer_text: Children and young people with medical needs, including epilepsy, should have a healthcare plan in place.
