question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why the Review of Grading in Modern Languages at GCSE and A-level has not been published since being commissioned in 2020; and (ii) when it will be published.
answer_text: The language reports submitted by the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) are currently being reviewed by my Department.  Officials are scoping the curriculum policy implications of these reports, as part of a wider approach to enhance and strengthen modern languages moving forward, in conjunction with CCEA colleagues.  The aim is to recognise and promote the importance of languages and their place within the curriculum, and in doing so, attempt to address the decline in uptake at key stage 4 and post-16 levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will collect data in relation to the number of health and social care staff that have received dementia-specific training.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of our Health and Social Care workforce having access to education and training in relation to Dementia to enable them to provide safe and effective care.The Dementia Education and Learning Framework was launched in September 2016.  This framework supports the training requirements of staff working within dementia care across the range of health and social care sectors.  There are numerous Dementia specific training resources utilised within Health and Social Care Trusts. Storage of data and information relating to training will vary, for example line managers will keep records in relation to staff training and development.  The Clinical Education Centre and the Learning Management System (also known as LearnHSCNI) also retain information regarding staff training provided through their services. Figures of attendance are also collated through the various training teams across disciplines of courses including: The Dementia bus, Dementia dictionary, Vocational skills and simulation training.The recently established Dementia Project Board and associated Education and Training work-stream are currently scoping Dementia Education provision across the region. All five HSC Trusts are represented on the Project Board and the Education and Training Workstream.The Education and Training workstream have begun to map current provision, gaps in provision and potential solutions to collating information relating to the wide variety of education and training staff can avail of.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Eastern Transport Plan 2035.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to work with councils and stakeholders on the development of the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP).  Results of a public engagement exercise that was undertaken to launch the ETP, were recently published on the Department’s website. Building on the transport challenges identified through public and stakeholder engagement, a set of Belfast City Centre proposals have been developed and are currently being finalised. Testing of the proposals will commence shortly with public engagement planned for 2025. Simultaneously, work is ongoing to develop transport proposals covering the entire five council ETP area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has made an initial assessment of the impact developer contributions will have on wastewater system capacity.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether all planned works for Phase 1 of the A5 Western Transport Corridor will commence at the same time.
answer_text: My Department has programmed for main construction works on the Strabane to Omagh section to commence next year with the Omagh to Ballygawley section to commence one year later.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions have taken place with the Minister for Infrastructure to address the shortfall in funding for Northern Ireland Water.
answer_text: I have had recent discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the funding requirements for NI Water. During a recent meeting in September in relation to the in-year budget position, the issue of the capital funding position of NI Water was discussed. I have also had a further meeting with the Minister for Infrastructure to discuss specifically the question of developer contributions as a means of funding water and wastewater infrastructure. At that meeting, the Infrastructure Minister outlined that legislative change would be required before NI Water could introduce developer contributions in relation to water and wastewater infrastructure and that he is currently considering how best to proceed. I recognise that a lack of water and wastewater capacity is acting as a constraint on development in many locations, particularly in relation to social housing, and this does need to be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions have taken place with the Minister for Infrastructure to address the shortfall in funding for Northern Ireland Water.
answer_text: I have had recent discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the funding requirements for NI Water. During a recent meeting in September in relation to the in-year budget position, the issue of the capital funding position of NI Water was discussed. I have also had a further meeting with the Minister for Infrastructure to discuss specifically the question of developer contributions as a means of funding water and wastewater infrastructure. At that meeting, the Infrastructure Minister outlined that legislative change would be required before NI Water could introduce developer contributions in relation to water and wastewater infrastructure and that he is currently considering how best to proceed. I recognise that a lack of water and wastewater capacity is acting as a constraint on development in many locations, particularly in relation to social housing, and this does need to be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when improvements to the Newbuildings to Strabane section of the A5 Western Transport Corridor will commence.
answer_text: My officials continue to make good progress in addressing the issues raised in the Planning Appeals Commission report in regards this section of the road and I remain fully committed to all 85 kilometres of the scheme in line with my statutory duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work will commence on the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: My Department has programmed for main construction works on the Strabane to Omagh section to commence next year with the Omagh to Ballygawley section to commence one year later.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Omagh bypass will be included in Phase 1 of the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: Yes, my decision to proceed relates to the length of road from Strabane to Ballygawley and therefore bypasses the town of Omagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department enforces cycle lanes.
answer_text: The Traffic Management (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 gives my Department the power to issue Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) when a vehicle is detected parked on a legislated Mandatory Cycle Lane. Mandatory Cycle Lanes are sited at carriageway (road) level and are separated from the rest of the carriageway by a solid white line or by a raised traffic island.Advisory Cycle Lanes also sited at carriageway (road) level but separated from the rest of the carriageway by a broken white line are not enforceable. However, if there is a parking restriction marked on the carriageway, for example yellow lines, those restrictions also apply to the Advisory Cycle Lane.Cycle Tracks sited on footways are also not enforceable. However, if there is a parking restriction marked on the carriageway, for example yellow lines, this will also apply to the adjacent footway and any cycle tracks thereon.Vehicles parked on Advisory Cycle Lanes or Footway Cycle Tracks causing an obstruction, where no parking restrictions are marked on the carriageway, are a matter for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the introduction of an expert-led, Independent Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: We are currently considering the report from the SIB Review. The recommendations from the review will help to inform a revised paper on options for an Infrastructure Commission.  Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of budget for the works on the (i) A2 Clooney Road, Ballykelly; and (ii) A29 Ring Road, Coleraine, has been spent on (a) road resurfacing; and (b) active travel provision.
answer_text: The value of the Active Travel scheme on the A2 Clooney Road in Ballykelly is £2.6m and the value of the scheme on the A29 Ring Road in Coleraine is £0.8m.Provision of high quality active travel infrastructure along existing roads, in some cases means having to move and reprofile the existing carriageway, including replacing kerb lines and drainage.  This is the case on the Clooney Road and Coleraine Ring Road schemes where the carriageway needs to be realigned and reprofiled to facilitate the active travel infrastructure improvements. The resultant resurfacing is a necessary consequence of delivering the active travel improvements and cannot, therefore, be considered in isolation. The resurfacing element of the Clooney Road active travel scheme is estimated to be in the region £0.4m for the surface course material, with  the Coleraine Ring Road estimated to be in the region £0.2m. I can further confirm that the roads in question were not in a condition where carriageway resurfacing was considered a priority. It would not be practical to wait many years for road surfaces to deteriorate to the point where they would be prioritised for resurfacing before progressing any road improvement scheme, including improvements to active travel provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware of car insurers refusing to process changes to insurance policies when changing cars; and (ii) whether he or his officials will engage with the Association of British Insurers on this issue.
answer_text: Insurers can refuse to provide cover when changing cars as the risk originally insured will have changed and may now fall outside of their underwriters' criteria. Governance of the motor insurance industry is not a devolved matter; it falls within the remit of the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he is taking to strengthen the enforcement measures used to control unauthorised developments.
answer_text: The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 introduced new enforcement powers for councils and also increased several levels of fines with regards to suspected breaches of planning control.  Following consideration of the available evidence, the report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (RIPA) made numerous recommendations/actions covering key elements of the planning system.  In respect of enforcement my Department was not persuaded of the need to change the approaches to planning enforcement and this position has not changed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for an update on the draft Refugee Integration Strategy; and (ii) whether the long-term housing needs of displaced Ukrainians will be considered as part of the strategy.
answer_text: We are currently considering a draft Refugee Integration Strategy and intend to present a final version, and an associated Thematic Delivery Plan, to the Executive for agreement. In the meantime, work is ongoing under the auspice of the draft Strategy to continue to support refugees and asylum seekers here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to improve access to driving lessons for people with a disability.
answer_text: Whilst the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is responsible for regulating the Approved Driver Instructor (ADI) industry, it does not employ ADIs in the capacity of delivering driving instruction or have any influence over who they may present to driving test.Nidirect provides information for learner drivers, including information for those with a disability. It signposts learner drivers with disabilities to contact local specialist instructor/s at Disability Action and provides contact details to do so (Learning to drive with a disability | nidirect).Learner drivers with a disability will take the same driving test as all other candidates, regardless of impairment or condition.  Such candidates should inform the DVA of any disability at the time of booking their driving test and drivers with disabilities may be allocated additional time for their test, to accommodate specific requirements and/or allow for explanation of any adaptations. Driving examiners are specially trained to understand any specific needs that may arise from disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the public art that will be on view at the new Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) when it will be installed.
answer_text: Translink is committed to creating a number of public art pieces as part of Belfast Grand Central Station and its surrounding public realm. The first public art is now on display along the walls at the entrance to the station. This is the culmination of work with local communities and historians to capture the great stories of the area and celebrate the rich history through art and storytelling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his immediate plans to mitigate fuel poverty for people who are most vulnerable.
answer_text: The impacts of Fuel Poverty can be felt right across our society.  Living in a cold home creates health risks for people of all ages.  Cold and damp homes are linked to worsening respiratory conditions, cardiovascular diseases, poor mental health, dementia and hypothermia. This is particularly important for vulnerable people or those with existing health conditions. My officials are currently drafting a new Fuel Poverty Strategy which will set out a long-term strategic approach to reducing fuel poverty.  The new Fuel Poverty Strategy will take a needs-based approach to targeting support, with a focus on long term sustainable solutions to address the causes of fuel poverty and reduce the poor health outcomes associated with cold homes.  The strategy will contain some key themes including how government supports those living in or at risk of fuel poverty, improved collaboration to target the hard to reach and make best use of our resources, improve health outcomes and provide consumer protection through the energy transition.My Department continues to deliver the Affordable Warmth Scheme for owner occupiers and those in the private rented sector most at risk of fuel poverty. This application-based scheme provides low-income households with a range of heating and insulation measures to improve the thermal efficiency of their home. I am developing a new more ambitious fuel poverty intervention to replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when to comes to an end in March 2026.  This fuel poverty intervention will ensure people living in owner occupied or private rented homes and experiencing fuel poverty, receive vitally important energy efficiency work.  For those in need of immediate support, additional financial support is available through my Department’s Discretionary Support Service. This is unique to Northern Ireland and is there to provide support to people in financial need and those who require short term assistance in crisis or emergency situations.My Department’s ‘Make the Call’ service continues to support individuals and families who otherwise may be missing out on benefits they are entitled to.  My Department’s communication team regularly undertakes activities to promote Pension Credit to encourage pensioners to check their eligibility for pension credit and any other benefits. Our State Pension staff also triage customers who may be eligible to receive Pension Credit and introduce them directly to the Pension Credit team.The Northern Ireland Housing Executive also offers support with energy-efficiency advice that is targeted actively at vulnerable households and those that will benefit most from energy efficiency related advice.  As the One-Stop Shop for energy advice across all tenures in Northern Ireland, they offer information to help with energy efficiency, grants, signposting, renewable energy advice, how to switch energy provider, debt assistance, fuel poverty advice and how to benefit from oil-buying savings across NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legislative timescale for the increase of the maximum authorised weights for certain alternatively-fuelled vehicles and certain zero-emission vehicles, including when it will come into effect.
answer_text: I anticipate that these regulations will come into effect in the latter part of 2024 subject to no significant issues or barriers being identified by Departmental Solicitors Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when he will publish the consultation report on the Proposed Amendment to the Fees Charged by Approved Tachograph Centres; and (ii) when he expects to make a final decision on the outcome of the consultation.
answer_text: The consultation response report and my decision on the Proposed Amendment to the Fees Charged by Approved Tachograph Centres will be published in July.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her legislative plans will include protections for frontline transport workers.
answer_text: No-one should face mistreatment whilst doing their job and serving the public. Such attacks are wholly unacceptable and must not be tolerated.  A number of offences against the person already exist, with maximum sentences ranging from 6 months at the lower end of the scale up to a potential life sentence for the offence of causing grievous bodily harm with intent.These offences apply to all victims, regardless of their occupation. However, I have been shocked by reports of aggression being displayed against people who serve the public in a range of occupations, including transport workers.To recognise this increased risk the Department’s proposed Sentencing Bill will include a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing a service to the public or performing a public duty.  The new offence will have an increased maximum sentence of 12 months’ imprisonment in the magistrates’ courts and a two year maximum if tried on indictment.  In more serious cases the fact that the victim was such a worker will become a statutory aggravating factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost per kilowatt of (i) home charging; and (ii) public charging for electric vehicles.
answer_text: As the provision of home electricity is commercially led, my Department does not hold records of the cost per kilowatt of home electricity. There are a number of electricity providers operating in this market and they would be best placed to advise you of their individual home energy tariffs.Similarly, the provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is commercially led with responsibility for the setting of individual charging tariffs at publicly available charge points solely resting with the relevant charge point operator. There are more than fifteen charge point operators active in the market and they would be best placed to advise you of their tariffs at their publicly available electric vehicle charge points. My Department does not hold records of the cost per kilowatt of charging at publicly available electric vehicle charge points.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has conducted research on a biodiversity net gain scheme.
answer_text: No specific research has been carried out by my department on a ‘Biodiversity Net Gain’ (BNG) scheme, however, I am very keen for environmental protection and biodiversity enhancement to be central in all the activities of my Department.This is demonstrated by the extent of the Environmental Impact Assessment Report (EIAR) for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass, the changes I have introduced to the Department’s approach to grass cutting along our road network, and the production of a Biodiversity Strategy by Translink, which is an Arm’s Length Body of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of Financial Transactions Capital as a method to attract additional investment for capital projects.
answer_text: My Department has not been able to avail of Financial Transactions Capital (FTC). FTC must be used to provide a loan to or make an equity investment in a private sector entity and as such no opportunities have been identified within infrastructure that meet this requirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to publishing workforce statistics annually, including the number of registered children’s community nurses.
answer_text: Information on Health and Social Care (HSC) staff in post is published by my Department on an annual basis in the Health & Social Care Workforce Census at the link below. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/dhssps-statistics-and-research/workforce-statisticsThis official statistics report includes children’s community nurses within the category of paediatric nurse.  It is not possible to add a breakdown of setting (i.e. community) to this report.  However, the number of children’s community nurses can be obtained by my Department from the HSC Trusts.  The most recent information acquired is detailed in the response to AQW 9860/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to ensure that homeowners without a driveway can charge their electric vehicles from home.
answer_text: Over 80% of houses in the North have access to off street parking. In these situations, charging solutions are already available. However, for those who rely on on-street parking at their homes, home charging solutions are more problematic, primarily due to the issue of the charging cable needing to cross the footway. My officials are engaging with colleagues in other jurisdictions to evaluate the benefits of a number of pilot schemes being progressed in relation to cross pavement charging solutions.  Engagement is also taking place with key stakeholders and the Office Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV), which has recently announced the availability of grants for on street charging solutions. Guidance for applicants, local councils and Highway Authorities is currently being prepared by OZEV and my officials are considering how that guidance can be adapted to meet specific legislative arrangements here. Once available, this guidance will be used to assess if installations at particular locations can satisfy the conditions of the grant. Legislative arrangements contained in the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 detail where a street works licence is required to facilitate the safe channelling or suspension of a cable. This legislation is in place to protect public health and safety and to address ownership and liability issues.Due to the calling of a General Election the finalised guidance document from OZEV will be delayed. In the interim, my officials will continue to explore how legislative requirements can be satisfied and engage with stakeholders regarding charging solutions for those without a driveway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions have taken place with the Department of Business and Trade regarding Harland and Wolff’s application for an export development guarantee loan.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the decision was made to close road service operations and maintenance transport hubs, on a temporary basis, in the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council area; (ii) how long these closures will last; and (iii) whether the decision underwent rural proofing to ensure the temporary closure of services will not adversely impact local communities.
answer_text: Staff resourcing at industrial grade has been a recurring issue within my department, with my Operations and Maintenance teams witnessing a significant reduction in staff complement over a sustained period.Consequently, issues with recruitment and retention have made it difficult to maintain an industrial presence across all of my Department’s depots in the North. To address these issues, an internal review was completed to temporarily adjust the geographical spread and make best use of available resources, stabilising and securing the service delivery of core functions, whilst maintaining our health and safety obligations.	As an outworking of this review, routine services previously provided from Ballymoney depot have been temporarily transferred to the Northbrook Depot in Coleraine.  Ballymoney depot will still provide an out of hours function, such as winter and emergency services.The outworkings of the stabilisation review, which were subject to rural proof screening, seek to maintain a level of service for the next 12 to 18 months dependent on staff recruitment and retention.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline when the draft Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls will be approved.
To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to ensure children’s palliative care is sustainably funded.
answer_text: The Department and then Minister published the Paediatric Palliative and End of Life Care Strategy in 2016.  This strategy was completed after considerable consultation with all relevant stakeholders across the sector in NI and beyond to ensure it was as comprehensive as possible.  The Strategy includes 23 ambitious objectives that cover the full range of issues within paediatric palliative care, from diagnosis of a palliative or life limited condition through to bereavement support for families of those children who have passed away.Subsequent to the publication of the strategy and with the availability of Transformation Funding in 2019, a number of specific innovations were introduced in NI.  This has included the following: -The appointment of a paediatric palliative care lead in each Trust.  Funding for this is for one programmed activity per Trust per week which totals £48,597 regionally;the appointment of a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care consultant who was trained at Great Ormond Street but works at the RBHSC and has input across the region.  The funding for this is £120,000 per year;the appointment of Paediatric Palliative and Life Limited Nurse and service.  The nurse works in RJMH and RBHSC supporting staff and facilitating a move to non-hospital setting (home or hospice) for step down or step up care, and where appropriate, an end of life care service. As well as the specialist nursing role, this service has access to one 24/7 hospice bed or virtual hospice bed with support available for over 300 bed nights per year. Following a recent uplift in funding £204,515 is now available per annum.As well as the above a further £17,734 per annum was made available to offer administrative support to the Regional Paediatric Palliative Care consultant.  There was also a further £4,189 in 2021/22 to Southern Health and Social Care Trust in respect of a palliative medical lead for Paediatrics Strategy.In 2019, a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network (RPPCN) was created and this is chaired by a Paediatric Palliative Clinical Care lead.  The network is made up of key clinical and nursing leads from each Trust as well as the NI Children’s Hospice.  This network works closely together on two main areas.  Firstly, the network identifies the key priorities for delivery in line with the strategy and puts in place measures to deliver same and secondly the network acts as a vehicle for the region to identify areas of best practice and share clinical opinion on specific cases to ensure continuous improvement.To date the network has developed ante-natal care pathways, rapid discharge plans, advanced care plans and has also agreed pathways regarding pain relief, amongst others.  Currently the network is also working on other key issues including the provision of 24/7 end of life care as well as examining how to best develop closer ties with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland.To assist with the dissemination of clinical best practice a Project Echo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to address neurology waiting times in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider introducing regulations to ensure NHS dental patients continue to avail of NHS dentistry if their allocated dentist retires or moves practice.
answer_text: The legislation proposed would not be possible under the current General Dental Services Contract. General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are independent contractors and are not contractually obliged to register new Health Service (HS) patients. Individual GDPs will have varying number of HS patients on their lists, and many will have both HS and private patients.Usually when a GDP retires, moves practice, or ceases provision of HS dental care, their (former) practice will attempt to redistribute the patients on that GDPs list to other GDPs in the practice, or to other practices in the local area. A key part of the access issues facing patients has been the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment. In March, I announced a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, alongside a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years. I have also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether additional provision will be provided to GPs to guarantee aftercare for patients discharged from hospital.
answer_text: There is no additional provision being provided for GPs to provide post procedure aftercare to patients.  However primary and secondary care providers continue to work together to provide aftercare services which best suit the clinical requirement of post-operative patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the completion of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the total number of road fatalities in 2024.
answer_text: My assessment is, that the number of road deaths so far in 2024 is a matter of huge concern, and should be 	something that is shocking to us all as a society.	Last year, in 2023, 71 people sadly lost their lives on our roads – the highest number of road fatalities in eight 	years. As at the end of April, more people had died on our roads this year, than last. So, it is clear that more must 	be done.	As I’ve already stated, I am bringing the revised Road Safety Strategy to the Executive for their endorsement	after which we’ll collectively consider the appropriate actions to reduce the numbers killed on our roads.	Those actions need to cover a wide range of factors across engineering, education and enforcement which will 	create safe roads, safe vehicles and safe people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount his Department has spent on (i) planting trees; and (ii) maintaining trees, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Tree planting is usually carried out by my Department for one of two reasons; as part of a major works scheme, where it will be incorporated into the overall scheme costs; or as a remediation measure where it has been necessary to remove existing trees for safety reasons. The cost of remedial tree planting would be incorporated into general tree maintenance expenditure.Expenditure specifically incurred on planting trees and maintaining trees is not recorded separately in my Department’s finance system, however I can provide expenditure on Grass Cutting, Weed Control and Tree Maintenance for the past three completed financial years from 2020-21 to 2022-23 in Table 1 below.Grass Cutting, Weed Control and Tree Maintenance2020-212021-222022-23£m6.06.66.7Table 1

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to develop a pilot scheme for cross pavement charging solutions to enable electric vehicle owners to charge their vehicles at home.
answer_text: Over 80% of houses in the North have access to off street parking. In these situations, charging solutions are already available. However, for those who rely on on-street parking at their homes, home charging solutions are more problematic, primarily due to the issue of the charging cable needing to cross the footway. My Departmental officials are engaging with colleagues in other jurisdictions to evaluate the benefits of a number of pilot schemes being progressed in relation to cross pavement charging solutions.  Engagement is also taking place with key stakeholders and the Office Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) who have recently announced the availability of grants for on street charging solutions. Guidance for applicants, local councils and Highway Authorities is currently being prepared by OZEV.  Once available this will be used to assess if the arrangements for such installations at particular locations can satisfy the conditions of the grant and legislative arrangements contained in the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 where a street works licence is required to allow works to be carried out to facilitate the safe channelling or suspension of a cable. This legislation is in place to protect public health and safety and to address ownership and liability issues.Currently across the North there are around 540 publicly available charge points provided commercially by a range of Charge Point Operators which allow convenient access to charging for EV owners who are unable to charge their vehicles at home.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to develop a regional Geographic Information System map to show the locations of all (i) Tree Preservation Orders; and (ii) conservation areas.
answer_text: There is a requirement under the 2011 Act for all councils to keep registers containing information in relation to the Tree Preservation Orders (TPOs) within their council areas. I am encouraged to hear that the majority of Councils have created interactive Geographic Information System (GIS) maps which display the locations of TPOs within their council areas and some also detail conservation areas.I believe it is important to consider the protection of trees and as part of that seek to encourage greater openness and transparency through increased electronic mapping of TPOs and Conservation Areas and provision of online access to TPO registers.My Department will consider this matter and where appropriate  seek to engage and work with Councils to ensure that necessary statutory obligations for the protection of trees under relevant planning legislation, environmental directives and regulations are understood and therefore fully met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) which MOT test centres are equipped to provide an MOT for electric vehicles; (ii) how many MOTs were carried out on electric vehicles in the last 12 months; and, (iii) whether (a) Hydebank; and (b) Mallusk Test Centres have the capability to provide MOTs for electric vehicles.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) has 15 test centres across the North and all of them are equipped to test electric vehicles.  Hydebank, Mallusk and any future test centre constructed by the DVA will also be equipped to test electric vehicles.From 1 April 2023 to 31 March 2024, the DVA tested 8,065 electric vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will instigate a review of the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 (S.R. 2015 No. 70)  with regard to electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: The Planning (General Permitted Development) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2020 introduced new permitted development rights for electric vehicle charging points by amending Part 3 (Minor Operations) of the schedule to the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order 2015. These permitted development rights provide for the installation, alteration or replacement of wall mounted electric vehicle charging points in off-street parking areas (Class D), for the installation, alteration or replacement of free-standing electric vehicle charging points in off-street parking areas (Class E) and for the replacement of free-standing electric vehicle charging points in on-street parking areas (Class F), subject to certain conditions and limitations.I have currently no plans to review these permitted development rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why residents holding a Great Britain provisional license are unable to book a driving test with the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: In order to take a practical driving test in the North, it is a legal requirement that the candidate must be normally a resident, which means that a person lives here for at least 185 days in each calendar year.Learner drivers who hold a GB provisional driving licence are asked to successfully demonstrate residency here, through completion of a residency check, before their practical driving test can be booked.  This process is in place to ensure compliance with the Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) Regulations (NI) 1996 and the Road Traffic (NI) Order 1981, both as amended.  Once a residency declaration has been approved, a practical driving test can be booked. There are currently no plans to change the process for GB provisional driving licence holders living here to apply for a practical driving test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to adopt the Zero Emission Vehicle mandate during this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The Zero Emissions Vehicle Mandate was laid by the British Government, Scottish Government, and Welsh Senedd as the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes Order (VETS) in England, Scotland, and Wales in October 2023 and came into force in those jurisdictions from 03 January 2024. Due to the absence of the Assembly, it was not legally possible to introduce the legislation here. I am working with my officials to consider the best way forward and once I have made a decision, I will inform my Ministerial colleagues and Departmental Committee members.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the work of the Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Task-Force.
answer_text: My Department recognises the key role Electric Vehicles play in reducing transport emissions by switching fuels to low emission alternatives. While the roll out of EV Infrastructure in the North is commercially led, my Department, in chairing the EV Infrastructure Task Force, has a lead role in bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers and industry with a focus on delivering a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.The EV Infrastructure Task-Force provides a platform for the sharing of knowledge and expertise, exploring options to tackle some of the barriers which exist in providing EV Infrastructure and examining how demand for charging can be met across the Transport Sector.  In recognising the complexity and wide range of areas that need addressed over the next five years, the Task-Force agreed six key actions which were to be developed by my Department, the Department of the Economy, Charge Point Operators and other statutory bodies including the Consumer Council and the NI Utility Regulator.I have been encouraged by the progress made by the members of the Task-Force, in considering the priorities contained in the Action Plan, and the successful collaboration across sectors, which has led to significant interventions, such as the introduction of charging for electricity at publicly available charge points. The most recent available statistics indicate that there are now over 540 publicly available charge points here which represents an increase of 60% since August 2021. The Task-Force aims to meet quarterly to allow members to provide updates on the progress of the actions and review priorities, reacting to market developments and to consider the outcomes of formal consultation on key issues including the review of connection costs for Charge Point Operators entering the market.  My Officials have also been liaising with key stakeholders outside of this forum and are currently considering how the Task-Force can share the knowledge of the group and take the views of those who have entered the market and are making a significant contribution to the growth of charging infrastructure in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many illegal taxis have been identified by Driver and Vehicle Agency officials during spot checks in (i) Belfast; and (ii) across Northern Ireland, in the last three years.
answer_text: The number of illegal taxis identified by Driver and Vehicle Agency enforcement officers in the last 3 years where one or more criminal offences were subject to enforcement action is as follows:Calendar YearBelfastNorthern Ireland(Incl Belfast)2022265620236593 202417384Total1642331 From 1 January to 31 March 2024.The figures in the table are taken from DVA management information and are not within scope of DVA Official Statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his plans to appoint a chief psychological officer.
answer_text: The creation of a Chief Psychological Professions Officer within the Department is still under consideration as part of the wider Mental Health workforce review.  My Department will continue to work closely with psychology professions working in the HSC and with relevant representative bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of private diagnoses for adult ADHD (i) approved by a Health and Social Care Trust prescribing clinician for medication; and (ii) rejected by a Health and Social Care Trust prescribing clinician for medication, in the last 5 years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are currently no regionally commissioned services for the assessment and treatment of adult ADHD and no requirement for Health and Social Care Trusts to record if a person has ADHD. The information is therefore not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6963/22-27, how his officials will assess the level of need for adult ADHD services.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, currently there are no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision of services between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals having to wait longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability.  Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider subsidising advanced driving courses as an avenue for drivers to reduce their insurance premiums, whilst improving overall driving proficiency.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory role in providing a minimum standard of testing for all types of licences and these are met in the current testing regime.  Advanced driver schemes sit outside these statutory requirements.Completion of an advanced driving course is not a guarantee of lower car insurance premiums, as not all car insurance companies provide a discount for this additional training. If drivers do find a policy that will provide a discount, the original cost to the driver, self-funding the course, could be recouped in lower premiums over approximately 5 years.I support drivers improving their proficiency and road safety.  However, the advanced training scheme sits outside the statutory requirement and I therefore have no plans to subsidise these courses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what action he is taking to address the backlog of cases awaiting allocation to an Education Welfare Officer.
answer_text: My officials have been working with Education Authority officials over the past twelve months on the Business Case that is needed to resolve the on-going industrial action within the Education Welfare Service which is having a negative impact on waiting lists. The Business Case will need to be agreed by the Department of Finance and funding will be required to provide an increase in the size of the Education Welfare Service Team and to resolve the associated pay and grading issues.Following a Transformation programme conducted in 2021/22, the Education Welfare Service will be taking a much more proactive approach to the management of pupil attendance and all of the associated issues. Once implemented, a transformed Education Welfare Service will provide a timelier, consistent and more effective approach which will support schools and pupils in meeting their specific needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the recommendations made in the 2023 report Independent Review into Children’s Social Care in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The report of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services was published in June 2023 and a public consultation on 49 of 53 recommendations ran for 12 weeks between September and December 2023. Work to analyse consultation responses is underway and it is hoped that this will be completed by mid-late March.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide an update on a security budget for Members.
To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the Allergy Code; and (ii) whether his Department will endorse it.
answer_text: The Schools Allergy Code developed by The Allergy Team highlights the importance of the awareness of allergens, their management and responding to allergic reactions within a school setting. My department is currently finalising an update to the ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’ Guidance, which includes a section on Allergic Reactions/Anaphylaxis.  I have asked officials to consider the detail of the Schools Allergy Code for inclusion, if appropriate, in any associated resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.In October 2022 I published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) report on the scheme and asked my officials to carry out further work on three scenarios recommended within it. This work is expected to conclude in Spring 2024. Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will continue to co-fund the active school travel programme for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 5456/22-27 of 15 February 2024. My officials continue to liaise with the Public Health Agency who co-fund the Active School Travel Programme with my Department and await the outcome of these discussions.  Any decision on this programme in the next financial year will be subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the Northern Ireland allocation of the 2022 Public Courts Refurbishment Fund.
answer_text: The Executive did not receive a direct allocation for this Fund.Instead, the Executive’s Spending Review 2021 settlement included Barnett consequentials on the additional allocation provided to Department for Culture, Media and Sport for this Fund.The funding received was unhypothecated and formed part of the overall amount available in the 2022-23 and 2023-24 Budgets to be allocated in line with local priorities and needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans to promote waste recycling.
answer_text: I am committed to the promotion of recycling, but I cannot emphasise enough how important it is that we improve how we manage our resources. We need to put as much material back into the system for reuse and recycling as possible, facilitating a move to a low carbon circular economy. 		New targets have been set in legislation to drive forward these objectives including a requirement to recycle at least 70% of our waste by 2030 as set out in the landmark Climate Change Act. To achieve this, more ambition is needed. In Northern Ireland, we have already increased recycling rates from less than 10% in 2002 to over 50% in 2020. 		Now, we need to go further.		With that in mind, I have approved launch of a consultation, titled “Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI”, which launched on Thursday 7th March and is available for responses on the DAERA website. The consultation sets out 26 proposals for change.  		My department is seeking views from all stakeholders on these proposals, designed to help meet our new targets and I would urge everyone to respond.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6297/22-27, how many classrooms do not have a CO2 monitor.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. Since 2011 and 2014 all new build primary schools and post-primary schools respectively include CO2 monitors.In 2021, in response to the Covid-19 pandemic, the Education Authority acquired over 20,000 CO2 monitors and these were allocated to schools on request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6297/22-27, what action is taken when classrooms are found to have poor air movement and quality.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) working with my Department and school leaders, is committed to ensuring that there continues to be appropriate ventilation in schools in line with the latest public health guidance and advice from the Health and Safety Executive.  Guidance in relation to ventilation was issued to all schools on 7 December 2021.  Should it be proven that natural ventilation is deficient within a school, this should in the first instance be reported to the EA Maintenance helpdesk.  The EA will undertake remedial works to resolve the issue which may include repair or replacement of windows or installation of an air ventilation system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients are currently awaiting removal of Essure, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients currently awaiting removal of Essure is not available centrally in the Department.  HSC Trusts were asked to provide information and the following responses were submitted.There are currently less than five patients awaiting removal of Essure within Belfast Trust waiting lists.Within the Western Trust there are no patients waiting on Essure removal.The Northern and South Eastern Trusts were unable to provide a response as they advised that the coding for this procedure would not be specific to Essure and could be the reversal of any female sterilisation procedure.The Southern Trust ceased the completion of procedures using Essure in August 2017, following guidance issued by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued its guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the introduction of a default speed limit on restricted roads in Wales from 30mph to 20mph; and (ii) what plans he has to introduce similar legislation.
answer_text: My Officials have discussed the change of the default speed limit on restricted roads in Wales from 30mph to 20mph with their counterparts from the Welsh Government. While there are currently no plans to do this here, it is something that I am keeping under review as I recognise that appropriate speed limits are part of the solution to improving road safety and I firmly believe that attitudes to speeding have to change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of introducing 20mph zones in residential areas; (ii) how many of such zones were introduced during 2023-24; and (iii) how many his Department plans to introduce in 2024-25.
answer_text: The Department ran two trials on 20mph speed limits, one using signs only 20mph speed limits, and the other using a new arrangement of signs to introduce part-time 20mph speed limits at schools.  The Review Report on the two trials issued in March 2021 and is available: 20mph Speed Limit Trials - Review Report | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk) To date the Department has introduced 20mph zones at over 700 locations, six of which were put in place during 2023-24 financial year.  These are mostly in residential areas in conjunction with traffic engineering measures, such as road humps.  There are currently no 20 mph zones planned for the 2024-25 financial year.  In addition, over the past number of years the Department has introduced part-time 20mph limits at 215 schools and a review of this initiative is underway to determine if a further tranche is justified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Review of Neurology Services.
answer_text: The Review of Neurology services is progressing well and I expect a draft report to be finalised in the coming months. The review has been taken forward by a clinically-led Neurology Review Team. The Review Team has undertaken analysis in key areas of the service including user experience. This analysis, which includes a gap and constraints assessment, detailed data analyses and benchmarking against services elsewhere, is now complete.The Review Team has also considered how best to address pressures within the Neurology workforce. This has been across both specialist consultant and Nursing staff as well as the Allied Health Professional and Psychology workforces. Over the next number of months my Officials will be engaging with stakeholders to inform the final report of the Review. This will include patients, carers, charities and wider expert bodies including the Association of British Neurologists and the Getting It Right First Time Neurology Team. The Neurology Review Team will meet in April to review feedback and amendments to the report. Subject to that discussion, it is intended that the final report will be completed later in the spring.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the new budget necessary to deliver the aims of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer; and (ii) whether he has requested additional budget from the UK government for the Sub Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: My officials carried out a cost analysis in 2022/2023 which suggests around £120million will be required to modernise performance clubs to a similar standard as that enjoyed in neighbouring jurisdictions.Regarding Grassroots facilities, the ‘DCMS Grassroots Needs Assessment, Full Scoping Report’ published by the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport in June 2022 shows a Northern Ireland deficit on pitch provision at grassroots level which, was at the time of publication, expected to cost in the region of £60million to address. Further work to assess needs at grassroots level is on-going.The Irish Football Association are currently developing a business case for a National Training Centre. A final costed figure is not yet available.It should be noted that these are cost estimates and do not assume that funding can be made available at this level from any specific source, including central government. This is simply an estimation of overall potential need calculated at a specific point in time.I expect funding to come from a variety of sources, including match funding sources secured by footballing bodies and clubs themselves. I intend to seek my Executive colleagues’ agreement to allocate additional capital funding to this important Programme. I will also make a strong case for the UK to make a contribution to overall costs to modernise football facilities. I am engaging with the UK Sports Minister in early March.

answer_text: My Department’s funding to the Community Festivals Fund in the last three years is as follows:2020/21*2021/222022/23-£369k£369k*No funding was allocated in this financial year due to the pandemic.Further DfC funding has been provided directly by the Belfast Regeneration Directorate to the following festivals in the last three years: Name of EventDate of EventAmountOrangefest1/4/20 – 31/3/21£40,763Orangefest1/4/21 – 31/3/22£40,700Orangefest1/4/22 – 31/3/23£40,700NI Science Festival18/2/21 – 28/2/21£9,000Young at Art’s Belfast Childrens Festival 5/3/21 – 14/3/21£10,000

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many adults are on a waiting list for a diagnosis of ADHD.
answer_text: There are currently no regionally commissioned services for adult ADHD and therefore no requirement for HSC Trusts to manage a dedicated and comparable ADHD waiting list. The information is therefore not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to raise the minimum legal standard of food provision in care settings.
answer_text: I have no immediate plans to replace the Nutrition Strategy Promoting Good Nutrition: A Strategy for Good Nutritional Care for Adults in all Care Settings in Northern Ireland 2011 - 2015. The vision of the strategy was to improve the quality of nutritional care of adults in Northern Ireland in health and social care, through the prevention, identification and management of malnutrition in all health and social care settings including in their own homes.My officials are currently reviewing the care standards for both Nursing and Residential Care Homes, which will consider nutritional standards, following the publication of the Enhancing Clinic Care Framework (ECCF). This framework is to ensure those living in adult care homes have access to the clinical and wellbeing support they want, and need, to live healthy, fulfilling lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6020/22-27, how many children are still awaiting access to digital assistance tools.
answer_text: As part of “A Fair Start” Action Plan Key Area 3(x), the most recent delivery of digital devices took place in Autumn 2023 and was the third phase of the rollout of the programme. That particular phase was oversubscribed and consequently, requests for a further 1,907 devices have been placed on hold. The supply and distribution of further digital devices to schools is dependent upon additional capital budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions he is taking to counteract drivers being penalised for lapsed car tax, due to the unavailability of MOT appointments.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter and is administered and enforced by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. The DVA has no authority or influence with the DVLA on these matters.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers. The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; keep checking the booking system for an appointment before their MOT expires. If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many data scientists are currently employed in his Department.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure does not currently employ any data scientists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to improve ventilation in classrooms.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority (EA) work within the guidelines provided by the Department of Health concerning air quality and ventilation in schools and other public buildings.  Guidance on ventilation has been issued to all schools, providing advice on how to identify poorly ventilated areas and the measures that should be taken to improve ventilation if required.200 HEPA air filtration units were installed across the schools’ estate, and these are still in place.  If a school has a specific concern regarding ventilation, they should contact EA to address the matter directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department measures and monitors air quality in classrooms.
answer_text: Air quality in classrooms is monitored through the use of CO2 (carbon dioxide) monitors.  The monitors measure and monitor the air quality in classrooms by identifying the presence of CO2 which indicates poor air movement and quality.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of staffing levels in MOT test centres.
answer_text: The recruitment and retention of DVA vehicle examiners has become increasingly more difficult for several reasons including higher salaries being offered for qualified mechanics by other public and private sector organisations.  The number of applications received for vehicle examiner recruitment competitions has fallen and there has been a marked increase in the number of vehicle examiners who have resigned from the DVA to take up employment elsewhere.  As a result, the DVA has a number of vehicle examiner vacancies across its network of test centres.In 2023, a grading review for DVA vehicle examiners was conducted, recommending they should be regraded to the next higher grade.  A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition will launch on 19 February 2024, advertising the higher regraded salary. In addition, the Executive has agreed to allocate £688million to allow public sector pay deals to be progressed and we are committed to ensuring pay awards are implemented as quickly as possible.These measures will hopefully attract more applicants to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to refurbish MOT test centres.
answer_text: The DVA is investing in new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk, which are due to open in 2024 and 2025 respectively.   Further investment in new test centres will be subject to further consideration and funding availability. The DVA is also planning to replace existing vehicle testing equipment across its network of test centres, which is expected to take approximately two years.  This equipment replacement programme will require limited enabling construction works to be carried out at each test centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action the Housing Executive is has taking to limit the abandonment of single let properties.
answer_text: In respect of those placed in Single Lets, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has advised that there is periodic contact with the individual and their respective Housing Advisor. The Housing Executive also recently carried out a sample of assurance visits where they visited Single Let property to confirm that the applicant is occupying the accommodation.  Where no one was available at the time of the visit a letter was left asking the occupant to make contact and confirm occupancy. The outcome of this exercise will be considered by the Housing Executive to ascertain the value of conducting further assurance visits in the future. Providers also carry out periodic visits and would notify the Housing Executive if a property is abandoned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what digital assistance tools, to support home learning, have been (i) requested; and (ii) issued in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Within the last twelve months 5,023 digital devices have been provided to schools to assist with pupil learning across Phases 2 and 3 of the digital devices project. This delivers action key area 3(x) of the report “A Fair Start”. The breakdown by devices is as follows:PhaseiPadsChromebookMS Surface SETotal21,012 748 586 2,346 3724 677 1,276 2,677 Total1,7361,4251,8625,023 It is the responsibility of individual settings to determine whether these devices are used in school, at home or both.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will request the Audit Office to conduct a review of the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I am not requesting the Audit Office to conduct a review of the Planning Appeals Commission, the sponsorship of which is the responsibility of the Department of Justice. I want to work with all partners to support and deliver an efficient and effective planning system, although that is subject to all parts of the planning system being properly funded and resourced, including the Planning Appeals Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans he has to review the policy for vacant listed buildings.
answer_text: I have no current plans to review the exclusion that applies within non-domestic vacant rating (NDVR) for listed buildings, or where a building preservation notice applies. This is a long-standing feature of the non-domestic rating system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will appoint Special Commissioners, under the powers of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, as a means of addressing the resourcing challenges within the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I recognise the resourcing challenges of the Planning Appeals Commission and acknowledge that, under the Planning Act (NI) 2011, the Department of Infrastructure has powers to appoint independent persons to conduct certain matters such as hearings or examinations instead of the Commission. However, this is not necessarily a simple undertaking and I will be considering this issue with my officials in the coming weeks.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it does not hold this information on a parliamentary constituency basis, however the number of school crossing patrol officers by EA Locality Area is provided below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will amend the housing selection scheme to include domestic violence as a form of intimidation.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing in relation to the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. My officials are working with the Housing Executive to consider the way forward for the proposal relating to intimidation points. This includes independent research which is due to complete by the end of March 2024.Once options in relation to this proposal have been further developed, I will give them my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of spend on active travel, broken down by parliamentary constituency, for each of the last 5 financial years.
answer_text: Monitoring of Active Travel spend within the Department is not broken down by parliamentary constituency. The Member may wish to note that the active travel spend from financial years 2016/17 to 2020/21 is detailed in AQW 24320 /17-22 ( AIMS Portal (niassembly.gov.uk))   Approximately £12.9m capital and resource spend was recorded in 2021/22 and approximately £12.7million in 2022/23 against specific Departmental active travel projects and grants for councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding will be made available for overnight child respite care.
answer_text: The Department would acknowledge that there are currently significant pressures regarding the short break availability and respite care provision for families who have children with disabilities across Northern Ireland. There has been an increase in children reported to have a disability with a corresponding increase in demand.  Access to Short Break and Respite Services is assessed by each HSC Trust, and is determined by individually assessed need.In terms of improving service provision, the Department of Health has invested £750,000 in 2021/22 across the five HSC Trusts (£150,000 to each Trust recurrently) with a specific focus to increase capacity and expand the range of short break options for children with disability.With regard to planning for the future, a Task and Finish group has been established by the Department of Health to finalise the Adult Learning Disability Service Model and the Children with Disabilities (CWD) Framework.Further, the Department has established a Children’s Services Reform Process to reform Children’s Services. This reform process includes a workstream on Children with a Disability. This workstream will be looking specifically at the regional position in respect of short breaks and respite and will include a focus on demand and capacity. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning decisions.  Any commissioning of additional respite care provision will require additional funding.  Decisions in this regard will be subject to the future budget agreement.  As you will know there are many competing demands across health and social care in the context of a very challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action is being taken to curtail the abandonment of emergency temporary accommodation.
answer_text: In respect of confirming occupancy of temporary accommodation, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has advised that for those placed in hotels and B&B’s, accommodation providers send a daily email confirming those who stayed in their respective hotel/B&B for the previous day, and confirming any placements who vacated the previous day. Additionally, there is regular contact between the customer placed in a hotel/B&B and their respective Housing Advisor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to introduce a compulsory curriculum for schools and colleges on mental health and wellbeing.
answer_text: The mental health and wellbeing of all our pupils is a high priority for me.  The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 sets the curriculum out as Areas of Learning with contributory elements from Foundation Stage to key stage 4 (ages 4-16).  The Order also sets out statements of minimum contents which schools must teach.  However, a key feature of the curriculum is the flexibility that schools have to provide education over and above the statutory minimum that meets the needs of their pupils.  Within the statutory Northern Ireland Curriculum, mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing education is already included within the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding and Learning for Life and Work areas of learning.  Pupils are provided with opportunities to develop an understanding of how to maximise and sustain their mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing, including managing emotions and reactions to ongoing life experiences and enhancing self-awareness and developing coping strategies.  There are also a number of qualifications at key stage 4 and post-16 which provide opportunities for students to explore issues relating to self-esteem, mental health and wellbeing.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides curricular guidance and teaching support materials for use in schools on its Wellbeing Hub which contains a range of age-appropriate, up-to-date and relevant resources to support the emotional health and wellbeing of pupils.  Additionally, the joint Department of Education and Department of Health ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ was published in February 2021.  The main focus of the Framework is to provide overarching guidance to support those working in educational settings to help them promote emotional wellbeing and strengthen self-esteem and resilience in our children and young people.  It is designed to help promote wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach, and then to provide targeted support when needs are identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding will be made available for transitional mental health services for those aged 18 to 25.
answer_text: Action 13 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 commits to the development of proposals for transitions between Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) and Adult Mental Health Services. A paper outlining recommendations for reviewing the current transitions process in mental health services was completed in 2021.  Since then, there have been a number of more recent developments, including progress to establish a new Regional Mental Health Service, the Review of Children’s Services and the Gender Identity review, all of which will likely impact on the recommendations for future transition arrangements. Work on the development of a co-designed Mental Health Strategy Delivery Plan for 2024 – 2025 is currently underway.  Delivery of Action 13 will be considered in the context of this work, as well as the availability of additional funding to support implementation of the Mental Health Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will introduce the recommendations listed in the Stronger from the Start infant mental health campaign.
answer_text: Delivery of the objectives of the Stronger from the Start manifesto will be achieved through delivery of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 which has a clear focus on the promotion of prevention, early intervention, and wellbeing throughout a person’s whole life. Within the Strategy, there is recognition that the path to good mental health starts in infancy and it sets out a commitment to ensure the needs of infants are met in mental health services. In particular, Action 11 aims to ensure that the needs of infants are met in these services, and that the needs of vulnerable children and young people are met when developing and improving the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service, putting in place a ‘no wrong door’ approach.A three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Plan has also been developed through the Mental Health Strategy. One of the key actions within this plan is to ensure collective responsibility for promoting and supporting the emotional wellbeing of all our infants, children and young people.
